The Specific Objections — Answered One by One

When pressed for evidence, the critic reduces the “different Jesus” claim to a small handful of specific accusations. Each collapses on examination.

1. “Mormons say Jesus is the spirit brother of Lucifer.”

Meant to sound shocking. It is the straightforward consequence of biblical doctrine the critic himself struggles to articulate.

The Bible teaches that all human beings are the spirit offspring of God: “We are also his offspring” (Acts 17:28–29). God is “the Father of spirits” (Hebrews 12:9). At the creation, “all the sons of God shouted for joy” (Job 38:7). Christ is the firstborn (Colossians 1:15), “not ashamed to call them brethren” (Hebrews 2:11). We are “joint-heirs with Christ” (Romans 8:16–17).

The Bible also teaches that Lucifer existed in heaven before his fall (Isaiah 14:12; Luke 10:18; Revelation 12:7–9) and that fallen angels “kept not their first estate” (Jude 1:6) — a phrase that explicitly presupposes a prior existence.

Put those together honestly. If all spirits are God’s offspring, and Lucifer was a spirit who existed in God’s presence before his fall, then Lucifer is a spirit child of God who rebelled — our pre-mortal sibling who fell — and Christ, the Firstborn, is the Elder Brother who did not. This is not innovation. It is the plain reading of the text.

Asking whether the Elder Brother is the same as the fallen rebel is like asking whether George Washington is the same as Benedict Arnold because both were colonists. The category overlap is meaningless; the character difference is total. Christ is infinitely above Lucifer in office, glory, obedience, nature, and atoning power. The pre-mortal relation does not diminish Him by a degree.

The Colossians 1:16 objection. A careful critic answers: “‘By him were all things created… thrones, dominions, principalities, powers.’ If Christ created the angelic order, He cannot be a sibling of angels — that is creator-to-creature, not brother-to-brother.”

This misreads both the Greek and the doctrine. The verb ktizō (“created”) means to organize, found, establish, set in order — not exclusively to bring into being from nothing. Paul uses the same root in 2 Corinthians 5:17 of the believer as “a new creature” (ktisis), where no one imagines the believer was annihilated and re-made from nothing. He was reorganized in Christ.

Latter-day Saint doctrine matches the verse precisely: Christ, under the Father’s direction, organized the spirit creation — the thrones, dominions, and powers of the heavenly hierarchy. He is the Architect and Head of the angelic order. He is also, simultaneously, the Firstborn of the Father’s spirit children, sharing the same Father with the rest of the family. A firstborn son appointed by his father to order and rule the household is not thereby disqualified from being his siblings’ brother. He is set apart in office, not in family. Christ is the Firstborn (Col. 1:15), the Organizer (Col. 1:16), the One not ashamed to call us brethren (Heb. 2:11), the Captain of our salvation (Heb. 2:10), the Head of the body (Col. 1:18). The Bible holds all of these together. So does the Restoration.

2. “Your Jesus is a created being.”

A false charge that confuses LDS doctrine with the ancient heresy of Arianism. Arius taught that there was a time when the Son did not exist — that Christ was created out of nothing at a point in eternity past. Latter-day Saints reject this absolutely.

D&C 93:21, 29

“…I was in the beginning with the Father, and am the Firstborn… Man was also in the beginning with God. Intelligence, or the light of truth, was not created or made, neither indeed can be.”

The doctrine is explicit and load-bearing. Christ’s intelligence — the irreducible core of His being — was never created and cannot be. The Father organized that eternal intelligence into a spirit body, and Christ stands as the Firstborn of that organized spirit creation; but the substance of His being is co-eternal with the Father’s. There is no point in eternity past at which Christ did not exist. None.

A sophisticated critic responds: “You have just moved the Arian moment. The spirit-birth becomes the new creation point.”

The rejoinder fails. The organization of an eternal intelligence into a spirit body is not creation ex nihilo. It does not bring into existence something that did not exist. The intelligence that is Christ always was and always will be. Organization is not origination. The sculptor who forms eternal stone into a statue does not call the stone into being — the stone always was. The form is new; the substance is not. Arius claimed the substance of the Son was brought into being. Latter-day Saint doctrine claims the substance of the Son never was brought into being. The two positions are not adjacent. They are opposite.

Nicaea handles Christ’s beginningless existence through “eternal generation” — an eternal causation by the Father, required to preserve monotheism within Trinitarianism. The Restoration solves the same problem more cleanly: Christ’s intelligence was never caused. The Father’s priority is relational and organizational, not temporal or substantial. He is greater (John 14:28) because He is the Father of Christ’s spirit and the organizer of His glory — not because He existed when Christ did not. If anything, the LDS position is more thoroughly anti-Arian than Nicaea, because it removes the equivocation between “begotten” and “made” entirely.

3. “Your Jesus isn’t part of the Trinity.”

At least honest. Latter-day Saints do not affirm the Nicene-Athanasian formulation as developed in the fourth century. We affirm the Godhead — three distinct divine Persons (Father, Son, Holy Ghost) who are one in mind, will, glory, love, power, and purpose. We affirm exactly the biblical pattern. What we decline is the Greek metaphysical addition that they are “one substance” (homoousios) in a way that flattens their personal distinctness.

The word “Trinity” does not appear in the Bible. The Nicene Creed was composed in A.D. 325 and revised in 381; the Athanasian Creed is later still. These creeds use Greek philosophical categories — substance, essence, person — that are not biblical vocabulary. The Bible has Father, Son, and Holy Ghost. It has Christ’s prayer that His followers be “one” — “as we are one” (John 17:20–22), a unity of will and purpose. It has the baptism, where the Father speaks audibly, the Son stands in the Jordan, and the Holy Ghost descends as a dove — three distinct Persons in one scene (Matthew 3:16–17). It has Stephen seeing “the Son of man standing on the right hand of God” (Acts 7:55–56) — two distinct embodied Persons in glory.

Latter-day Saints affirm what the Bible affirms and decline what it never says. If declining a fourth-century philosophical formulation is the criterion for “not believing in the Jesus of the Bible,” then the apostles themselves — who never used “Trinity” or homoousios — fail the test. The standard is not Scripture. The standard is creedalism.

4. “Isaiah 43–44 rules out other divine beings entirely.”

The most-cited proof texts are Isaiah 43:10 (“before me there was no God formed, neither shall there be after me”), 44:6 (“I am the first, and I am the last; and beside me there is no God”), and 44:8 (“Is there a God beside me? yea, there is no God; I know not any”). They appear to settle the matter. They do not.

These are not statements of philosophical metaphysical monotheism. They are statements of covenant monolatry — God’s exclusive claim on the worship of His people, addressed to an Israel constantly tempted toward Baal, Asherah, Dagon, Marduk, Chemosh, and Molech. The “no other God” formula is spoken by the same divine voice that said “Thou shalt have no other gods before me” (Exodus 20:3) — a commandment that presupposes the existence of beings the Hebrews called gods while denying that any deserve Israel’s worship. The grammar of Isaiah’s declarations is the grammar of covenant exclusivity, not Aristotelian metaphysics.

The same Old Testament that gives Isaiah 44:6 gives Psalm 82:1 — “God standeth in the congregation of the mighty; he judgeth among the gods.” It gives Deuteronomy 32:8–9 in the oldest Hebrew, Septuagint, and Dead Sea Scrolls reading: God set the bounds of the peoples “according to the number of the sons of God.” It gives the divine council, the heavenly host, the sons of God who shouted at creation (Job 38:7). That the Hebrew Bible contains a heavenly assembly of elohim with Yahweh sovereign above them is now mainstream scholarship, documented at length even by evangelical scholars who study the divine council in the Hebrew Bible. (It must be said plainly, in fairness: those same evangelical scholars hold that the council’s elohim are created beings, a different species from the uncreated Yahweh, and they explicitly reject the Latter-day Saint application. The descriptive point — that the council exists in the text — is theirs and is solid. The conclusion drawn from it is contested, and the contest is exactly the substance of this debate.)

The decisive witness is Christ Himself. Accused of blasphemy for claiming divine sonship, He defended the claim from the Old Testament’s own “gods” language:

John 10:34–36

“Is it not written in your law, I said, Ye are gods? If he called them gods, unto whom the word of God came, and the scripture cannot be broken; Say ye of him, whom the Father hath sanctified, and sent into the world, Thou blasphemest; because I said, I am the Son of God?”

Christ cites Psalm 82:6 — “I said, Ye are gods” — as scriptural warrant for the legitimacy of His own divine title. His argument moves from lesser to greater: if Scripture could call recipients of God’s word “gods,” how much more the One the Father sanctified and sent. The “gods” language of the Old Testament carries genuine divine weight — Christ stakes His own defense on it. (Note: the argument does not depend on settling whether Psalm 82 addresses the divine council or Israel’s judges. On either reading, the term carries real divine freight, or Christ’s a fortiori would not function. The point is simply that the “no other God” of Isaiah cannot mean what the critic needs it to mean, since the same canon — and the same Christ — uses “gods” affirmatively.)

The one clause the monolatry reading must still face. A precise critic will narrow to a single phrase in Isaiah 43:10: “neither shall there be formed after me.” That clause, he says, is not about worship but about coming into existence — and that is exactly what an open divine economy seems to allow. This is the most resistant phrase in the passage and deserves a direct answer rather than a slide past it.
